aliases: Basmati rice with nuts and fruits
Ghee rice with dried fruits and nuts

english: Basmati rice with nuts and fruits
Ghee rice with dried fruits and nuts

region_of_origin: Peshawar, Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan / North-West Frontier culinary tradition
food_subgroup: Rice-based mixed dish with ghee, dried fruits, and nuts

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_00002694 covers pilaf as a category. No specific Peshwari Pulao entry exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A00LC covers rice dishes broadly. No specific Peshwari Pulao code exists; closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Peshwari Pulao or a sufficiently close pilaf/pulao parent concept.

main_ingredients: whole spices (cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, bay leaf)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of Peshwari Pulao is approximately 300 g, equivalent to roughly one heaped plate of fragrant basmati rice garnished with ghee-fried nuts and dried fruits.

nutrient_source.title: Pulao / Pilaf (basmati rice cooked with ghee, nuts, and dried fruits) — estimated from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods and USDA FoodData Central component data
nutrient_source.organization: ICMR-NIN (Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition) and USDA FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for Peshwari Pulao specifically. Values are estimated by combining ICMR-NIN data for cooked basmati rice (plain pulao) with USDA FoodData Central data for ghee, mixed nuts (almonds, cashews, pistachios), and dried fruits (raisins, apricots) at proportions typical of the dish (approximately 70% cooked rice, 8% ghee, 12% mixed nuts, 10% dried fruits). The composite estimate is consistent with published values for similar nut-and-fruit pilafs.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Basmati rice, cooked (analogous estimate for Peshwari Pulao)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Peshwari Pulao. GI of 52 is taken from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for cooked basmati rice (GI ~52, low category). The addition of ghee and nuts is expected to slightly lower the effective GI due to fat and protein content slowing gastric emptying; therefore GI 52 is a conservative upper-bound estimate for this dish. GL per serving = (52 × 31.5 × 300) / (100 × 100) = 49.1.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content (~180 mg/100 g; ~540 mg per 300 g serving) and ghee-derived saturated fat
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to 150–200 g and request reduced salt preparation, as a full 300 g serving contributes approximately 540 mg sodium — roughly 23% of the 2,300 mg daily limit — alongside saturated fat from ghee that may compound vascular risk.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycemic load (~49 per 300 g serving) despite low GI basmati rice base
type2_diabetes.guidance: Although basmati rice has a low GI (~52), the large carbohydrate load per restaurant serving (approximately 94.5 g carbohydrate per 300 g plate) produces a substantial glycemic load; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit portions to 150 g or pair with a high-fibre vegetable side to blunt postprandial glucose excursions.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from ghee (~3.5 g/100 g; ~10.5 g per serving) partially offset by cardioprotective unsaturated fats from mixed nuts
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The ghee content contributes approximately 10.5 g of saturated fat per 300 g serving, which approaches the recommended daily limit for saturated fat in cardiovascular disease management; however, the mixed nuts (almonds, cashews, pistachios) provide monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fats that partially offset this risk, and portion control to 150 g is advisable for individuals with established cardiovascular disease.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Peshwari Pulao is a fragrant, festive rice dish originating from the Peshawar region of present-day Khyber Pakhtunkhwa, Pakistan, and is a celebrated preparation within the broader North Indian and Pakistani culinary tradition. It is prepared by cooking long-grain basmati rice using the absorption method after tempering whole aromatic spices — cardamom, cloves, cinnamon, and bay leaf — in generous quantities of clarified butter (ghee). The dish is distinguished from plain pulao by the incorporation of dried fruits such as raisins and dried apricots, and a variety of roasted or ghee-fried nuts including almonds, cashews, and pistachios, which are folded into or scattered over the cooked rice. The result is a mildly sweet, aromatic, and richly textured rice dish with a golden hue from the ghee and a complex interplay of savoury, sweet, and nutty flavours. The texture contrasts fluffy, separate rice grains with the chewiness of dried fruit and the crunch of nuts. In Singapore, Peshwari Pulao is served at Indian and Pakistani restaurants as a premium rice accompaniment or as a standalone main course. Nutritionally, it is a moderate-energy dish (~210 kcal/100 g) with meaningful fat content from ghee and nuts, including saturated fat (~3.5 g/100 g) and beneficial unsaturated fats. The basmati rice base provides a low glycemic index (~52), making it preferable to short-grain white rice for glycemic management, though the large carbohydrate load per restaurant serving warrants portion awareness for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes. The nuts contribute protein, magnesium, zinc, and vitamin E. Clinically, the dish is moderate concern for hypertension due to sodium and saturated fat, and moderate concern for cardiovascular disease due to ghee-derived saturated fat, partially mitigated by the nut content.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for Peshwari Pulao as a named dish in ICMR-NIN or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are estimated from component-level data for cooked basmati rice, ghee, mixed nuts, and dried fruits at typical proportions. GI is an analogous estimate from basmati rice data in the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are composite estimates derived from ICMR-NIN (cooked basmati rice / plain pulao) and USDA FoodData Central (ghee, almonds, cashews, pistachios, raisins, dried apricots) at approximate proportions: 70% cooked rice, 8% ghee, 12% mixed nuts, 10% dried fruits. Actual restaurant values will vary by recipe. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the component sources used for this estimate and is not expected to be a significant contributor in this dish. 3. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the ICMR-NIN source for cooked basmati rice and was not reliably available across all nut components used. 4. trans_fat_g is 0.0; ghee contains negligible industrially produced trans fats; naturally occurring ruminant trans fats (CLA) are present at trace levels but are reported as 0.0 per USDA data for ghee. 5. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0; this is a plant-based and ghee dish with no meat, fish, or egg components; vitamin B12 is naturally zero in this formulation. 6. GI of 52 is a conservative analogous estimate from basmati rice (University of Sydney GI Database); the actual GI of the complete dish may be marginally lower due to fat and protein from ghee and nuts slowing gastric emptying. 7. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_100g × serving_g) / 10000 = (52 × 31.5 × 300) / 10000 = 49.1. 8. The dish is served at Indian House / Dumpukht Grills & Curry, Boat Quay, Singapore (field survey source). 9. Multilingual names follow Singaporean dish rules (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as this is an Indian cuisine dish served in Singapore.
